compiled_state.title: As a developer, I can trust that sub-agent dependent skills exit immediately when invoked in sub-agent context
compiled_state.author: Joe Oakhart

comments.body:
## Description

**What**: Design the sub-agent guard block pattern and add it to the first 9 sub-agent dependent skills: sprint, debug-everything, brainstorm, preplanning, implementation-plan, design-wireframe, design-review, roadmap, plan-review.
**Why**: These skills dispatch sub-agents to function properly. When invoked from within a sub-agent, they silently degrade because nested sub-agent dispatch fails. An early exit prevents wasted work and confusing failures.
**Scope**:
- IN: Design the guard block text for sub-agent dependent skills, write a test file that verifies all 18 target skills contain the guard, add guard to 9 skills listed above
- OUT: User-interaction dependent skills (Story 2), remaining 6 sub-agent dependent skills (Story 2), documentation updates (Story 3)

## Done Definitions

- When this story is complete, each of the 9 target skills contains a guard block in its preamble that checks Agent tool availability and exits with a clear error message if unavailable
  <- Satisfies: "Each of the 18 target skills exits immediately with a clear error message when it detects it is running as a sub-agent"
- When this story is complete, a test file exists that verifies all 18 target skills contain the expected guard pattern (tests for the 9 remaining skills will be RED until Story 2 completes)
  <- Satisfies: "The guard block follows a consistent pattern across all 18 skills"
- When this story is complete, the guard block appears before any other skill logic in each modified skill
  <- Satisfies: "The guard appears in the skill preamble (before any other logic) so no work is wasted before exiting"
- When this story is complete, unit tests are written and passing for all new or modified logic

## Considerations

- [Testing] Single test file should grep all 18 target skills for guard pattern to enforce consistency
- [Reliability] Verify no existing workflow legitimately invokes these skills from sub-agent context
- [Maintainability] Guard block pattern established here is reused verbatim in Story 2
comments.author: Joe Oakhart

comments.body:
## Adversarial Review Finding (accepted)

**implementation-plan needs graceful degradation, not hard exit**: /dso:implementation-plan is legitimately invoked as a sub-agent from /dso:sprint. A hard "exit immediately" guard would break sprint's workflow entirely. implementation-plan should either be removed from the hard-exit guard list (it already has its own sub-agent handling in Step 6) or use a graceful degradation pattern like fix-bug instead of a hard exit. This must be resolved during guard pattern design.

## Additional Consideration (adversarial review)

- [Testing] Test file must support two guard variant patterns from the start: one for sub-agent dependent skills (Agent tool check) and one for user-interaction dependent skills (orchestrator signal phrase check). Story 2 adds the second variant — Story 1's test design must accommodate both.
comments.author: Joe Oakhart

comments.body:
## Revision: Adversarial finding superseded

The adversarial review finding about implementation-plan needing graceful degradation is superseded. Implementation-plan gets a hard exit guard like all other skills. Sprint will be updated (in a separate story) to invoke implementation-plan via Skill tool instead of Task tool, so the guard will never trigger in the normal sprint flow. The guard protects against any other orchestrator attempting to dispatch implementation-plan as a sub-agent.
comments.author: Joe Oakhart

comments.body: Fixed: All 9 sub-agent dependent skills now have SUB-AGENT-GUARD blocks and tests pass.
comments.author: Joe Oakhart
